data.description: Glassy headers have become a core part of the “slick startup” UI toolkit, but they’re all missing that final 10% that really makes it shine. In this tutorial, you’ll learn how to create the most realistic lush frosted glass anywhere on the internet.

(function() {
  const DEFAULT_MODE = 'light';

  let colorMode;
  let cookies = document.cookie ? document.cookie.split('; ') : [];

  cookies.some((cookiePair) => {
    const [key, value] = cookiePair.split('=');

    if (key === 'saved-color-theme') {
      colorMode = value;
      return true;
    }
  });

  if (!colorMode) {
    colorMode =
      window.matchMedia('(prefers-color-scheme: dark)')?.matches
        ? 'dark'
        : 'light';
  }

  // Ignore any values other than 'light' and 'dark';
  if (!['light', 'dark'].includes(colorMode)) {
    colorMode = DEFAULT_MODE;
  }

  if (colorMode === DEFAULT_MODE) {
    return;
  }

  // At this point, we’ve identified their preferred color mode, and it doesn't match the color mode used during SSR. We need to overwrite all of the colors.
  const colorMap = {"--color-text":"hsl(210deg 10% 90%)","--color-background":"hsl(210deg 15% 6%)","--color-blurred-background":"hsl(210deg 15% 6% / 0.75)","--color-muted-background":"hsl(210deg 38% learning</a></nav><div class="l1mdfuoz"></div></div><a id="introduction" class="wcmb8ol"><span class="wlgbkjj">Introduction</span></a><p class="prg881n">One of my all-time favourite CSS tricks is using <code class="i165vvr1">backdrop-filter: blur()</code> to create a frosted glass effect. I use it in just about every project I work on, including this blog!</p>
<p class="prg881n">Here’s a quick demo, to show what I’m talking about:</p>

<p class="prg881n">This effect helps us add depth and realism to our projects. It’s lovely.</p>
<p class="prg881n"><strong>But when I see this effect in the wild, it’s almost always missing some crucial optimizations.</strong> A couple of small changes can make our frosted glass <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">so</em> much more lush and realistic!</p>
<p class="prg881n">In this post, you’ll learn how to make the slickest frosted glass ever ✨. We’ll also learn quite a bit about CSS filters along the way!</p>

<h2 id="css-filters" data-element-type="ContentHeading" class="hrk0sy8"><a id="css-filters-1" href="#css-filters-1" class="aax5dne"><span class="aieubfy"><svg viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" style="width:21px;height:21px;opacity:1" class="s1gvsyfu"><line x1="2" y1="8" x2="22" y2="8" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="2" y1="16" x2="22" y2="16" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="10" y1="2" x2="6" y2="22" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="18" y1="2" x2="14" y2="22" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line></svg><span class="wlgbkjj">Link to this heading</span></span></a>CSS filters</h2>
<p class="prg881n">To briefly explain the underlying concept: CSS gives us quick and easy access to SVG filters via the <code class="i165vvr1">filter</code> property.</p>
<p class="prg881n">For example, we can give elements a Gaussian blur with <code class="i165vvr1">filter: blur()</code>:</p>

<p class="prg881n">There are lots of fun filter options, the sorts of things you’d find in image-editing software. Like, rotating the hue of all the colors:</p>

  );--background-color:var(--color-background);--base-handle-size:16px;--track-size:3px" type="range" id=":Rgteiavfelb:_sepia" min="0" max="100" class="i1i8zjy7" value="0"></div></div></div></div></div>
<p class="prg881n">Pretty neat, right?</p>
<p class="prg881n">Things get even cooler with <code class="i165vvr1">backdrop-filter</code>. This property lets us apply these same filters to the stuff <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">behind</em> a given element.</p>
<p class="prg881n">For example:</p>
<!-- --><div data-use-adaptive-colors="true" data-demo-unit="true" data-control-position="bottom" class="o1m9cdug d16kq653"><div class="w1v90hn6"><div class="w1qszusq"><div class="isvxvbh"><img src="/images/backdrop-filter/tokyo.jpg" loading="lazy" class="tvcyp0e"><div style="top:80%;left:50%;--scale:2;-webkit-backdrop-filter:sepia(50%) brightness(130%);backdrop-filter:sepia(50%) brightness(130%)" class="ctfyewy"></div></div><div class="ck6v2m7"></div></div><div class="d1j2qstn"><div class="s4h798d"><div style="opacity:1" class="h1pypve8"><label for=":Rh5eiavfelb:_sepia" class="lpsyt9t">Sepia<!-- -->:</label><span class="w1194iyg">50%</span></div><div class="cxwqm2n"><input style="--handle-color:var(--color-gray-900);--handle-focus-color:radial-gradient(

<p class="prg881n">In this demo, the <code class="i165vvr1">.magic-ring</code> element sits in front of a photo (<a rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank" class="a1tdgj4y s1j91s21" href="https://unsplash.com/photos/assorted-signages-on-buildings-nTBW1cOY1qI"> <span class="p7a6phg">source<svg viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" style="width:0.7em;height:0.7em;opacity:1" class="s14qet46 s1gvsyfu"><mask id="external-icon-mask-:Rn17eiavfelb:"><rect x="0" y="0" width="24" height="24" fill="white"></rect><rect x="10" y="0" width="16" height="14" fill="black"></rect></mask><rect x="3" y="6" width="15" height="15" rx="2" mask="url(#external-icon-mask-:Rn17eiavfelb:)"></rect><path d="M 10 14 L 20 4 h -6 h 6 v 6" stroke="currentColor" class="amgfren"></path></svg><span class="wlgbkjj">(opens in new tab)</span></span></a>). It uses the <code class="i165vvr1">backdrop-filter</code> property to apply some filtering to everything behind it, which can be used for some pretty artistic effects.</p>
<p class="prg881n">In practice, I pretty much only use <code class="i165vvr1">backdrop-filter</code> for one use case: blurring everything behind an element, usually a header, to create the “frosted glass” effect I mentioned earlier:</p>

<p class="prg881n">Alright. Let’s talk about the thing most developers miss.</p>
<h2 id="the-issue" data-element-type="ContentHeading" class="hrk0sy8"><a id="the-issue-2" href="#the-issue-2" class="aax5dne"><span class="aieubfy"><svg viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" style="width:21px;height:21px;opacity:1" class="s1gvsyfu"><line x1="2" y1="8" x2="22" y2="8" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="2" y1="16" x2="22" y2="16" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="10" y1="2" x2="6" y2="22" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="18" y1="2" x2="14" y2="22" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line></svg><span class="wlgbkjj">Link to this heading</span></span></a>The Issue</h2>
<p class="prg881n"><strong>Here’s the problem:</strong> The <code class="i165vvr1">backdrop-filter</code> algorithm only considers the pixels that are <i>directly behind</i> the element.</p>
<p class="prg881n">For filters like <code class="i165vvr1">brightness</code> or <code class="i165vvr1">hue-rotate</code>, that makes perfect sense. With blur, though, we actually want to consider pixels that are <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">near</em> the element too.</p>
<p class="prg881n">This is one of those things where a demo is worth a thousand words. Check out the difference:</p>
<!-- --><div data-use-adaptive-colors="true" data-demo-unit="true" data-control-position="top" class="o1m9cdug d16kq653"><div class="w1v90hn6"><div style="grid-template-columns:1fr" class="d1j2qstn"><div class="i1df5wci s360ott"><div id=":Rhneiavfelb:_mode" class="w73h7tw"><div class="r1cct0ab"><button value="default" style="opacity:1" class="t6358bx"><span style="height:4px;filter:saturate(100%);opacity:1" class="b10n1zbt"></span><span class="tglc2q3">Default</span></button><button value="fixed" tabindex="-1" style="opacity:0.65" class="t6358bx"><span style="height:2px;filter:saturate(0%);opacity:0.4" class="b10n1zbt"></span><span class="tglc2q3">Tweaked</span></button></div></div></div></div><div class="wr5us8n s1hfctpl d16kq653"><div class="hhqru8"><div class="g8qz4zc"></div></div><div style="animation-play-state:running" class="b1uj7lhw"></div></div><div class="awqze7l"><button style="--pb-background-color:hsl(175deg 60% 25%);--pb-highlight-color:hsl(168deg 70% 35%)" data-discouraged="false" class="b1rsl36b w19mrn5v"><span style="order:1" class="iwv8n0j c1rpf4ut"><span class="i1qpbbb9"><span style="transform:translate(0px, 0px)
         rotate(0deg)
         scale(1);backface-visibility:hidden" class="i1m1cbkp"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="20" height="20" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round" class="lucide lucide-pause"><rect x="14" y="4" width="4" height="16" rx="1"></rect><rect x="6" y="4" width="4" height="16" rx="1"></rect></svg></span></span></span><span style="order:2" class="c7sw782">Play/Pause Animation</span></button></div></div></div>
<p class="prg881n">By default, the gaussian blur algorithm is applied to all of the pixels behind the element. This means that if a big colorful element is <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">near</em> the element, it won’t have any effect.</p>
<p class="prg881n">That’s not really how frosted glass works in real life though. Light bounces off of objects and then goes through the glass. It looks <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">so much better</em> when the blurring algorithm includes nearby content.</p>
<p class="prg881n">Unfortunately, this isn’t something we can configure directly. Instead, we need to be a bit crafty.</p>
<p class="prg881n">Here’s the code:</p>

  header {
    position: relative;
  }
  .backdrop {
    position: absolute;
    inset: 0;
    height: 200%;
    backdrop-filter: blur(16px);
    mask-image: linear-gradient(
      to bottom,
      black 0% 50%,
      transparent 50% 100%
    );
  }
</style>

<header>
  <div class="backdrop"></div>

<div class="ball"></div></textarea><style>
/**
 * Reset the text fill color so that placeholder is visible
 */
.npm__react-simple-code-editor__textarea:empty {
  -webkit-text-fill-color: inherit !important;
}

/**
 * Hack to apply on some CSS on IE10 and IE11
 */
@media all and (-ms-high-contrast: none), (-ms-high-contrast: active) {
  /**
    * IE doesn't support '-webkit-text-fill-color'
    * So we use 'color: transparent' to make the text transparent on IE
    * Unlike other browsers, it doesn't affect caret color in IE
    */
  .npm__react-simple-code-editor__textarea {
    color: transparent !important;
  }

  .npm__react-simple-code-editor__textarea::selection {
    background-color: #accef7 !important;
    color: transparent !important;
  }
}

<p class="prg881n">It looks complicated, but the principle isn’t too scary.</p>
<p class="prg881n">If we want the blur to consider elements nearby, we need to <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">extend</em> that element so that it covers those elements. Then, using a mask, we trim the excess away, so that it’s visually the same size as we originally intended.</p>
<div style="min-width:32px;min-height:32px" class="w1fjy7al"></div>
<p class="prg881n"><strong>Let’s walk through it step by step.</strong> First, we have a header with a backdrop blur:</p>

<p class="prg881n">Because the red ball isn’t behind the header at all, it isn’t being considered by the blurring algorithm, and so we don’t get that soft red glow. We need to <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">extend</em> the header so that it covers at least some of the ball.</p>
<p class="prg881n">Rather than give the <code class="i165vvr1"><header></code> an explicit height (which would lock us into a specific size, rather than a dynamically-calculated one), let’s move the <code class="i165vvr1">backdrop-filter</code> to a child element, and set that child element to be twice as large as its parent:</p>

<p class="prg881n">What you see here depends on your browser. On Firefox and Safari, this works great! But sadly, it doesn’t work on Chrome. There’s no soft red glow.</p>
<p class="prg881n">I think it’s an order-of-operations issue. In Chrome, the overflow trimming occurs <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">before</em> the filters are applied, so when the blurring algorithm is executed, the content has already been hidden.</p>
<p class="prg881n">For the same reasons, we can’t use <code class="i165vvr1">overflow: clip</code> or <code class="i165vvr1">clip-path</code>, but fortunately, we <i>can</i> use <code class="i165vvr1">mask-image</code>. The masking algorithm happens <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">after</em> the filters, in all browsers. ✨</p>
<p class="prg881n">Masking is a huge topic which is well beyond the scope of this tutorial, but the basic idea is that we can specify how transparent parts of an element should be. For example, if our mask is an opaque circle in a transparent box, that opaque shape can be applied to any other element:</p>
<!-- --><div class="w175hke3"><div class="cqyj72i"><div class="lma5bw3">Element</div><img src="/images/backdrop-filter/tokyo.jpg" class="i1uvbyvl"></div><div class="sfuo6ds">+</div><div class="cqyj72i"><div class="lma5bw3">Mask</div><div class="sivonfy"></div><img src="/images/backdrop-filter/tokyo-mask.png" class="m1i4un07 ivyzkeh"></div><div class="sfuo6ds">=</div><div class="cqyj72i"><div class="lma5bw3">Result</div><div class="sivonfy"></div><img src="/images/backdrop-filter/tokyo-masked.png" class="ivyzkeh"></div></div>
<p class="prg881n">Most commonly, masks are images in a format that supports transparency (like <code class="i165vvr1">.png</code> or <code class="i165vvr1">.gif</code>), but we can also use <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">gradients</em> as masks. For example, we can fade an image from opaque to transparent:</p>

<p class="prg881n">Our mask doesn’t <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">look</em> like a gradient, does it? I typically picture gradients fading smoothly from one color to the next.</p>
<p class="prg881n">It might feel like an <button class="t1cz8cv8"><span class="w4pilfo">illegal building technique<span class="t4hbmw3">?</span></span></button><span data-use-adaptive-colors="true" id=":Rjjeiavfelb:" data-direction="n" role="tooltip" style="flex-direction:column" class="p1lgzcrp l17lpp8k"><span class="p833mxt"><span class="pj0hw36"><span class="c1fvuxyj">Term from the LEGO world, referring to assembling LEGO bricks in a way that the manufacturer did not intend.</span></span></span><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="24" height="6" fill="none" viewBox="0 0 24 6" style="--origin-y:0%" class="s1vq0yeh s1k9z937"><path d="
        M 0 0
        C 6 0
          7.199999999999999 6
          12 6
        C 16.8 6
          18 0
          24 0
        Z
      "></path></svg></span>, but this is what we need in this case. Our gradient is solid black from 0% to 50%, then it instantly becomes transparent for the final 50%.</p>
<p class="prg881n"><strong>Why 50%?</strong> We set <code class="i165vvr1">height</code> to 200%, so that <code class="i165vvr1">.backdrop</code> will always be twice as tall as its container. The percentages inside <code class="i165vvr1">mask-image</code>’s gradient are relative to the <i>current element’s size</i>.</p>
<p class="prg881n">For example, if our <code class="i165vvr1"><header></code> is 200px tall, our <code class="i165vvr1">.backdrop</code> will grow to 400px (200% of its parent). Then, our mask will show the first 50% of this element (0px to 200px), and hide the rest (200px to 400px).</p>
<p class="prg881n">Here’s the code again. Feel free to experiment with it, to develop your intuition for what’s happening:</p>

/**

.npm__react-simple-code-editor__textarea:empty {

/**
 * Hack to apply on some CSS on IE10 and IE11
 */
@media all and (-ms-high-contrast: none), (-ms-high-contrast: active) {
  /**
    * IE doesn't support '-webkit-text-fill-color'
    * So we use 'color: transparent' to make the text transparent on IE
    * Unlike other browsers, it doesn't affect caret color in IE
    */
  .npm__react-simple-code-editor__textarea {
    color: transparent !important;
  }

  .npm__react-simple-code-editor__textarea::selection {
    background-color: #accef7 !important;

<p class="prg881n">Here’s what happens when I try on desktop:</p>
<div style="min-height:205px"></div>
<p class="prg881n"><strong>Here’s the problem:</strong> the <code class="i165vvr1">mask-image</code> property will <i>visually</i> hide parts of an element, but the element is still there. We’re not able to click on the text because that <code class="i165vvr1">.backdrop</code> is extending out and covering it!</p>
<p class="prg881n">Fortunately, it’s an easy fix:</p>

<p class="prg881n">The <code class="i165vvr1">pointer-events</code> property allows us to specify that an element should be ignored when resolving click/touch events. <code class="i165vvr1">mask-image</code> makes the backdrop invisible, and <code class="i165vvr1">pointer-events: none</code> makes the backdrop <button class="t1cz8cv8"><span class="w4pilfo">incorporeal<span class="t4hbmw3">?</span></span></button><span data-use-adaptive-colors="true" id=":R24heiavfelb:" data-direction="n" role="tooltip" style="flex-direction:column" class="p1lgzcrp l17lpp8k"><span class="p833mxt"><span class="pj0hw36"><span class="c1fvuxyj">Something that can be seen but not felt, like a mirage or a ghost</span></span></span><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="24" height="6" fill="none" viewBox="0 0 24 6" style="--origin-y:0%" class="s1vq0yeh s1k9z937"><path d="
        M 0 0
        C 6 0
          7.199999999999999 6
          12 6
        C 16.8 6
          18 0
          24 0
        Z
      "></path></svg></span>.</p>
<p class="prg881n">This is another reason why <code class="i165vvr1">.backdrop</code> needs to be a <i>child</i> element. We don’t want the <code class="i165vvr1"><header></code> itself to ignore clicks, since it typically has navigation links. We want to target the frosted glass element specifically.</p>
<h3 id="flickering-top" data-element-type="ContentHeading" class="h1uetiv1"><a id="flickering-top-4" href="#flickering-top-4" class="aax5dne"><span class="aieubfy"><svg viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" style="width:18px;height:18px;opacity:1" class="s1gvsyfu"><line x1="2" y1="8" x2="22" y2="8" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="2" y1="16" x2="22" y2="16" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="10" y1="2" x2="6" y2="22" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="18" y1="2" x2="14" y2="22" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line></svg><span class="wlgbkjj">Link to this heading</span></span></a>Flickering top</h3>
<p class="prg881n">By extending the glassy backdrop <i>below</i> the header, we can ensure that the blurring algorithm takes it into consideration even before that element reaches the header.</p>
<p class="prg881n">But what about when things leave the top of the viewport?</p>
<p class="prg881n">Things aren’t quite so nice. <strong>Scroll down slowly in this demo:</strong></p>

<p class="prg881n">Notice that weird goopy flickering, at the very top of the viewport?</p>
<div data-include-caption="false" data-alignment="center" style="--max-width:365px;aspect-ratio:365 / 60;outline:1px solid var(--color-content-outline)" class="w1hban68"><video autoplay="" loop="" muted="" playsinline="" src="/images/backdrop-filter/flickering-top-ff.mp4" aria-label="Close-up of the above demo, showing the issue at the top" class="vsmrqjy"></video><button style="opacity:0" class="ps5yn9o"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round" class="lucide lucide-pause"><rect x="14" y="4" width="4" height="16" rx="1"></rect><rect x="6" y="4" width="4" height="16" rx="1"></rect></svg></button></div>
<p class="prg881n">It’s the same issue as before. The gaussian blur algorithm is only considering the pixels directly underneath it. When a yellow longboard is scrolled out of view, for example, that data is no longer factoring into the blur algorithm, causing those unnatural color flickers.</p>
<p class="prg881n"><strong>Unfortunately, we can’t re-use our solution here.</strong> As far as I can tell, elements outside the viewport are <i>never</i> considered by <code class="i165vvr1">backdrop-filter()</code>, even if the elements are layered correctly.</p>
<p class="prg881n">The best solution I’ve found for this solution is to add a gradient that covers the flickering:</p>

<p class="prg881n">Until now, the <code class="i165vvr1">.backdrop</code> element has been fully transparent; we haven’t applied a <code class="i165vvr1">background</code> at all. This gradient makes it opaque at the very top, blocking the flickering colors from view, but fading to transparent, to show the frosted glass effect.</p>

<h2 id="browser-support" data-element-type="ContentHeading" class="hrk0sy8"><a id="browser-support-6" href="#browser-support-6" class="aax5dne"><span class="aieubfy"><svg viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" style="width:21px;height:21px;opacity:1" class="s1gvsyfu"><line x1="2" y1="8" x2="22" y2="8" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="2" y1="16" x2="22" y2="16" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="10" y1="2" x2="6" y2="22" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="18" y1="2" x2="14" y2="22" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line></svg><span class="wlgbkjj">Link to this heading</span></span></a>Browser support</h2>
<p class="prg881n"><code class="i165vvr1">backdrop-filter</code> has been around in all major browsers for a number of years now; <a rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank" class="a1tdgj4y s1j91s21" href="https://caniuse.com/css-backdrop-filter">according to<!-- --> <span class="p7a6phg">caniuse<svg viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" style="width:0.7em;height:0.7em;opacity:1" class="s14qet46 s1gvsyfu"><mask id="external-icon-mask-:R1cu5eiavfelb:"><rect x="0" y="0" width="24" height="24" fill="white"></rect><rect x="10" y="0" width="16" height="14" fill="black"></rect></mask><rect x="3" y="6" width="15" height="15" rx="2" mask="url(#external-icon-mask-:R1cu5eiavfelb:)"></rect><path d="M 10 14 L 20 4 h -6 h 6 v 6" stroke="currentColor" class="amgfren"></path></svg><span class="wlgbkjj">(opens in new tab)</span></span></a>, it’s above 97% support as I write this in December 2024. For our main optimization, we also need <code class="i165vvr1">mask-image</code>, which is <a rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank" class="a1tdgj4y s1j91s21" href="https://caniuse.com/mdn-css_properties_mask-image">almost as well<!-- --> <span class="p7a6phg">supported<svg viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" style="width:0.7em;height:0.7em;opacity:1" class="s14qet46 s1gvsyfu"><mask id="external-icon-mask-:R1du5eiavfelb:"><rect x="0" y="0" width="24" height="24" fill="white"></rect><rect x="10" y="0" width="16" height="14" fill="black"></rect></mask><rect x="3" y="6" width="15" height="15" rx="2" mask="url(#external-icon-mask-:R1du5eiavfelb:)"></rect><path d="M 10 14 L 20 4 h -6 h 6 v 6" stroke="currentColor" class="amgfren"></path></svg><span class="wlgbkjj">(opens in new tab)</span></span></a>, sitting at 96.3%.</p>
<p class="prg881n">Both properties require a <code class="i165vvr1">-webkit</code> prefix for some browsers, but most CSS tooling will add this for you automatically.</p>
<p class="prg881n">At the bottom of this blog post, I’ll include the full copy-ready code, which uses feature queries to make sure that older browsers still have a usable experience. They won’t get the frosted glass effect, but everything will still be readable and usable.</p>

17h.01"></path></svg></div><strong id="aside-weird-firefox-quirks" class="t1xqw7pl">Weird Firefox quirks</strong><div class="c690lmu"><p class="prg881n">While working on this blog post, I ran into a <a rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank" class="a1tdgj4y s1j91s21" href="https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=1803813">known<!-- --> <span class="p7a6phg">bug<svg viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" style="width:0.7em;height:0.7em;opacity:1" class="s14qet46 s1gvsyfu"><mask id="external-icon-mask-:Rb4mebeiavfelb:"><rect x="0" y="0" width="24" height="24" fill="white"></rect><rect x="10" y="0" width="16" height="14" fill="black"></rect></mask><rect x="3" y="6" width="15" height="15" rx="2" mask="url(#external-icon-mask-:Rb4mebeiavfelb:)"></rect><path d="M 10 14 L 20 4 h -6 h 6 v 6" stroke="currentColor" class="amgfren"></path></svg><span class="wlgbkjj">(opens in new tab)</span></span></a>: <code class="i165vvr1">backdrop-filter</code> stops working in Firefox on a <code class="i165vvr1">position: sticky</code> element if an ancestor has both <code class="i165vvr1">overflow</code> and <code class="i165vvr1">border-radius</code> set.</p><p class="prg881n">A reader also emailed to let me know about another weird issue they ran into in Firefox. Sometimes, the blurring would flicker in and out, showing a crisper shape. They fixed this by setting <code class="i165vvr1">overscroll-behavior: none</code>.</p><p class="prg881n">These issues both seem pretty niche, but if you run into them, hopefully this saves you some time!</p></div></aside>
<h2 id="glassy-edge" data-element-type="ContentHeading" class="hrk0sy8"><a id="glassy-edge-7" href="#glassy-edge-7" class="aax5dne"><span class="aieubfy"><svg viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" style="width:21px;height:21px;opacity:1" class="s1gvsyfu"><line x1="2" y1="8" x2="22" y2="8" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="2" y1="16" x2="22" y2="16" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="10" y1="2" x2="6" y2="22" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="18" y1="2" x2="14" y2="22" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line></svg><span class="wlgbkjj">Link to this heading</span></span></a>Glassy edge</h2>
<p class="prg881n">As if this stuff wasn’t complicated enough already, Artur Bien came up with an extra twist; we can create the illusion of a 3D piece of glass by adding a <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">second</em> blurred element with different filter settings:</p>

    hsl(48deg 100% 75% / 1),

  ),

    from 90deg at 50% 50%,

  );--background-color:var(--color-background);--base-handle-size:16px;--track-size:3px" type="range" id=":Rmheiavfelb:_radius" min="0" max="16" class="i1i8zjy7" value="8"></div></div></div><div class="w1icjxur s1hfctpl d16kq653"><div class="hv1pb5o"><div class="bunns4"></div><div style="--thickness:3px;--radius:8px" class="e9evofd"></div></div><div style="animation-play-state:running" class="b19n0c7a"></div></div><div class="ayr5vrn"><button style="--pb-background-color:hsl(175deg 60% 25%);--pb-highlight-color:hsl(168deg 70% 35%)" data-discouraged="false" class="b1qtwz37 w19mrn5v"><span style="order:1" class="iwv8n0j c1rpf4ut"><span class="i1qpbbb9"><span style="transform:translate(0px, 0px)

<p class="prg881n">Isn’t that lovely?!</p>
<p class="prg881n">Here’s how this works: The bottom edge is a separate DOM node with <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">its own</em> <code class="i165vvr1">backdrop-filter</code>. I find it looks better with a smaller blur radius (eg. 8px in the bottom edge, 16px in the main backdrop), and with an extra <code class="i165vvr1">brightness</code> filter to really make it pop. ✨</p>
<p class="prg881n">The code for this is a bit gnarly 😅. I’ve done my best to explain it in the comments below:</p>

class="npm__react-simple-code-editor__textarea" autocapitalize="off" autocomplete="off" autocorrect="off" spellcheck="false" data-gramm="false"><style>
  header {
    --thickness: 4px;
    position: sticky;
    top: 0;
    /*
      Very opaque, for unsupported browsers.
      Overwritten in the feature queries below.
    */
    background: hsl(0deg 0% 100% / 0.95);
  }

  /*
    This code gets applied for folks using
    browsers that support backdrop-filter.
  */
  @supports
    (backdrop-filter: blur(16px)) or
    (-webkit-backdrop-filter: blur(16px))
  {
    header {
      background: hsl(0deg 0% 100% / 0.5);
    }

    .backdrop {
      position: absolute;
      inset: 0;
      /*
        These prefixed properties are usually
        added automatically by tooling:
      */
      -webkit-backdrop-filter: blur(16px);
      backdrop-filter: blur(16px);
      background: linear-gradient(
        to bottom,
        hsl(0deg 0% 95%),
        transparent 50%
      );
      pointer-events: none;
    }

    .backdrop-edge {
      position: absolute;
      left: 0;
      right: 0;
      bottom: 0;
      height: var(--thickness);
      background: hsl(0deg 0% 100% / 0.1);
      -webkit-backdrop-filter: blur(12px) brightness(0.96);
      backdrop-filter: blur(12px) brightness(0.96);
      transform: translateY(100%);
      pointer-events: none;
    }
  }

  /*
    This code gets applied for folks using
    browsers that support mask-image. This
    adds the "consider near elements"
    optimization discussed in this article.
  */
  @supports
    (mask-image: none) or
    (-webkit-mask-image: none)
  {
    .backdrop {
      height: 200%;
      -webkit-mask-image: linear-gradient(
        to bottom,
        black 0% 50%,
        transparent 50% 100%
      );
      mask-image: linear-gradient(
        to bottom,
        black 0% 50%,
        transparent 50% 100%
      );
    }
    .backdrop-edge {
      height: 100%;
      inset: 0;
      -webkit-mask-image: linear-gradient(
        to bottom,
        black 0,
        black var(--thickness),
        transparent var(--thickness)
      );
      mask-image: linear-gradient(
        to bottom,
        black 0,
        black var(--thickness),
        transparent var(--thickness)
      );
    }
  }
</style>

<header>
  <div class="backdrop"></div>
  <div class="backdrop-edge"></div>
</header>
<div class="ball"></div></textarea><style>
/**
 * Reset the text fill color so that placeholder is visible
 */
.npm__react-simple-code-editor__textarea:empty {
  -webkit-text-fill-color: inherit !important;
}

/**
 * Hack to apply on some CSS on IE10 and IE11
 */
@media all and (-ms-high-contrast: none), (-ms-high-contrast: active) {
  /**
    * IE doesn't support '-webkit-text-fill-color'
    * So we use 'color: transparent' to make the text transparent on IE
    * Unlike other browsers, it doesn't affect caret color in IE
    */
  .npm__react-simple-code-editor__textarea {
    color: transparent !important;
  }

  .npm__react-simple-code-editor__textarea::selection {
    background-color: #accef7 !important;
    color: transparent !important;
  }
}

class="npm__react-simple-code-editor__textarea" autocapitalize="off" autocomplete="off" autocorrect="off" spellcheck="false" data-gramm="false"><style>
  .backdrop {
    position: absolute;
    inset: 0;
    height: 200%;
    backdrop-filter: blur(16px);
    mask-image: url(#frostyGlassMask);
  }
  /*
    Our SVG mask fills the space,
    to match the same size
  */
  .svg-mask {
    position: absolute;
    inset: 0;
  }
</style>

<div class="main">
  <header>
    <div class="backdrop"></div>
    <svg
      class="svg-mask"
      width="100%"
      height="100%"
      preserveAspectRatio="none"
    >
      <mask id="frostyGlassMask">
        <rect
          class="header-rect"
          width="100%"
          height="100%"
          fill="white"
          rx="8"
          ry="8"
        />
      </mask>
    </svg>
  </header>
  <div class="ball-container">
    <div class="ball"></div>
    <div class="ball"></div>
    <div class="ball"></div>
  </div>
</div></textarea><style>
/**
 * Reset the text fill color so that placeholder is visible
 */
.npm__react-simple-code-editor__textarea:empty {
  -webkit-text-fill-color: inherit !important;
}

/**
 * Hack to apply on some CSS on IE10 and IE11
 */
@media all and (-ms-high-contrast: none), (-ms-high-contrast: active) {
  /**
    * IE doesn't support '-webkit-text-fill-color'
    * So we use 'color: transparent' to make the text transparent on IE
    * Unlike other browsers, it doesn't affect caret color in IE
    */
  .npm__react-simple-code-editor__textarea {
    color: transparent !important;
  }

  .npm__react-simple-code-editor__textarea::selection {
    background-color: #accef7 !important;
    color: transparent !important;
  }
}

metadata.author: Josh W. Comeau
metadata.description: Glassy headers have become a core part of the “slick startup” UI toolkit, but they’re all missing that final 10% that really makes it shine. In this tutorial, you’ll learn how to create the most realistic lush frosted glass anywhere on the internet.
metadata.og:description: Glassy headers have become a core part of the “slick startup” UI toolkit, but they’re all missing that final 10% that really makes it shine. In this tutorial, you’ll learn how to create the most realistic lush frosted glass anywhere on the internet.
